answers.question: What is the overall goal, purpose or aim of this study?
answer: Diagnostic: estimating current disease status or distinguishing between patients with and without a disease
answers.raw_answer: ["Diagnostic: estimating current disease status or distinguishing between patients with and without a disease"]
choices_ids: Diagnostic: estimating current disease status or distinguishing between patients with and without a disease
Screening: identifying asymptomatic or general-population individuals who are likely to have or develop a disease
Prognostic: predicting future clinical outcomes for patients with a particular disease
Etiologic: identifying factors associated with or causing disease or health outcomes
Treatment effect: evaluating the effect of an intervention or comparing interventions
Descriptive: describing disease prevalence, incidence, characteristics, or trends without primarily making predictions
Other: the goal of the study does not fit any of the other categories
Unknown from this paper
answer_label: Diagnostic: estimating current disease status or distinguishing between patients with and without a disease

answers.question: What information about the dataset is provided in this study, for example regarding the features, provenance, data quality, missing values, and so on?
answers.answer:
The study provides the following dataset information:  
- **Provenance**: Images were sourced from public databases (Hellenic Dermatology Atlas, Danderm.dk) with permission, as well as from dermatologists in India.  
- **Data quality**: 17,718 raw images were initially collected, but 310 were discarded during preprocessing due to poor resolution or multiple lesions.  
- **Data split**: Of the remaining 17,408 images, 12,350 were used for training and 3,068 for testing.  
- **Imbalance handling**: The dataset included 1,990 non-specific images and 15,418 images across 40 disease categories. A weighted focal loss function was employed to address class imbalance.  
- **Preprocessing**: Images were annotated, segmented, and processed to isolate lesions, removing irrelevant features like rulers or surgical markings.  

Missing values or specific feature details (e.g., metadata) are not explicitly mentioned.
answers.raw_answer:
The study provides the following dataset information:  
- **Provenance**: Images were sourced from public databases (Hellenic Dermatology Atlas, Danderm.dk) with permission, as well as from dermatologists in India.  
- **Data quality**: 17,718 raw images were initially collected, but 310 were discarded during preprocessing due to poor resolution or multiple lesions.  
- **Data split**: Of the remaining 17,408 images, 12,350 were used for training and 3,068 for testing.  
- **Imbalance handling**: The dataset included 1,990 non-specific images and 15,418 images across 40 disease categories. A weighted focal loss function was employed to address class imbalance.  
- **Preprocessing**: Images were annotated, segmented, and processed to isolate lesions, removing irrelevant features like rulers or surgical markings.  

Missing values or specific feature details (e.g., metadata) are not explicitly mentioned.

[Material and methods > Generation of the CNN-based algorithm] The CNN architecture used was a modified version of Densenet161 with optimized augmentation and inference pipelines.<sup>16</sup> These optimizations were derived through rigorous experiments and were attuned to clinical skin images. All training and testing images were annotated and segmented so a bounding box surrounded lesion of interest and irrelevant features such as rulers and surgical markings were discarded. The 17 718 raw images were sourced from public databases (http://www.hellenicderma tlas.com/en and http://www.danderm.dk/atlas), after obtaining permission from them, as well as images from dermatologists in India. Of these, 310 images were discarded during the preprocessing stage due to poor resolution or multiple lesions. Out of the remaining 17 408 images, 1990 images belonged to the non-specific category and 15 418 images were within the 40 selected disease categories (Table 1). These images were split into five equal parts (folds) and five iterations of training and validation were performed in a manner so that within each iteration, a different fold of the data was held-out for validation while the remaining fourfolds were used for learning. The training images were 12 350 and testing images were 3068. Since the dataset was imbalanced, a custom modified version of the weighted focal loss function was used to train the network.

answers.question: What type of data or data modalities are used in this study?
answer: Image data: data consisting of images
choices_ids: Tabular data: structured data represented as rows and columns
Time-series data: data points that have temporal relationships between them
Image data: data consisting of images
Text data: data consisting of (free) text
Audio data: data consisting of audio recordings
Video data: data consisting of video recordings
Multimodal data: data consisting of multiple modalities (e.g., tabular, text, image, audio)
Other: the data or data modalities do not fit any of the other categories
Unknown from this paper
answer_label: Image data: data consisting of images
chunks_str: [Material and methods > Clinical validation study] Figure 1 This flowchart depicts the different steps in data collection, algorithm generation, algorithm testing, app generation and app validation in clinical studies. Pangti et al.

[A machine learning-based, decision support, mobile phone application for diagnosis of common dermatological diseases > Results] In this work, clinical images of the patients’ lesions were taken by seven different dermatologists in primary, tertiary and urban practice in settings of varying lighting conditions, backgrounds, with mobile phones having different camera settings, resolution, zoom and focus. However, there was not much difference in the accuracy of the app in three different settings suggesting that such an app can be a robust clinician-friendly technology under varying conditions. The scarcity of training data, especially clinical images from different ethnicities, is considered the biggest challenge in developing optimal deep learning-based skin disease classifiers. The public dermatology archives like ISIC largely contain skin cancer dermoscopic images rather than clinical images of common diseases and are highly skewed in terms of image numbers for different diseases. With the use of locally generated data, we were able to counter the issue of class imbalance to some degree by selective collection from patients. Also, dataset bias and racial bias have been described, arising from the reuse of the same public datasets from white-skinned individuals for training, evaluating or comparing models in nearly all studies.<sup>30</sup> Therefore, we used several thousand new patient images for training and all distinct images for validation. To enable our app to make decisions on clinically relevant features, we also trained our model on images of normal skin from individuals as a control.
